title: [Bug]: The podman container update command doesn't seem to work.
body:
### Issue Description

The `podman container update` command doesn't seem to work.



### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman container create --name testing registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/leap /bin/sh -c 'sleep 3600'`
1. `podman container start testing`
1. `podman container inspect -f '{{.HostConfig.BlkioWeight}}' testing`
1. `podman update --blkio-weight 10 testing`
1. `podman container inspect -f '{{.HostConfig.BlkioWeight}}' testing`

NOTE: On Ubuntu 22.04 I had to use `--memory-swap` because ` --blkio-weight` returned an error:
```
2023-01-23T11:07:22.541171Z: opening file `io.weight` for writing: No such file or directory
Error: `/usr/bin/crun update --resources=/var/tmp/podman2282833966 5a3aceff3fa774c996ee639d29028e4b155e923202483600d710f92e1c4efba1` failed: exit status 1
```

### Describe the results you received

The `inspect` command returns the same value before and after `podman update`, which didn't return any error.


### Describe the results you expected

The `inspect` command should return the changed value after `podman update`, otherwise the latter command should fail with an error message.


### podman info output

```shell

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Tried on both:
- openSUSE Tumbleweed (QEMU VM w/ 2 vCPU's & 1536M RAM) running as root
- Laptop with Ubuntu 22.04 using [Kubic repo](https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/devel:kubic:libcontainers:unstable/xUbuntu_22.04) running as non-root.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
This is reproducible with the latest state on main (ebc754ff1f1e89fdc59821af5ba36e85cb0796f8), insofar that neither `--blkio-weight` nor `--memory-swap` succeed, I always receive a:
```
❯ ./bin/podman update --memory-swap 10 testing
2023-01-23T17:24:26.354800Z: error opening file `/run/user/1000/crun/3d028d7c4d98b76a8754a44ad7f3b32556e878a3d749167d21f5fbbdc72a7ee6/status`: No such file or directory
Error: `/usr/bin/crun update --resources=/var/tmp/podman2277971324 3d028d7c4d98b76a8754a44ad7f3b32556e878a3d749167d21f5fbbdc72a7ee6` failed: exit status 1
```

@ricardobranco777 Did you run the commands as root?

> @ricardobranco777 Did you run the commands as root?

On Tumbleweed I ran the commands as root.  On Ubuntu as non-root.

`--blkio-weight` cannot work as non-root according to the manpage, so the failures that we both got in rootless mode are to be expected.

This appears to be an issue with podman's inspect code: if I understand the code correctly, then `podman create` or `podman run` will create the `OCIConfigPath` json file into which the container settings are written. That file does not appear to get updated by `podman container update` (at least I did not find that in the code), however the blkio weight is set correctly:
```ShellSession
# cat /sys/fs/cgroup/machine.slice/libpod-$(podman inspect -f {{.Id}} testing).scope/container/io.bfq.weight
default 10
```

@ricardobranco777 do you know if this used to work? If yes, with which version?

> @ricardobranco777 do you know if this used to work? If yes, with which version?

No.  I just expect it to work like Docker, which updates the HostConfig.

> ```
> cat /sys/fs/cgroup/machine.slice/libpod-$(podman inspect -f {{.Id}} testing).scope/container/io.bfq.weight
> ```

The path is different for me:
- Tumbleweed: `/sys/fs/cgroup/machine.slice/libpod-$(podman inspect -f {{.Id}} testing).scope/`
- Ubuntu: `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/user.slice/libpod-$(podman inspect -f {{.Id}} testing).scope/`

I strongly believe that Podman should behave like Docker here and modify `OCIConfigPath`

This is probably a bug in the update code, because setting `--blkio-weight` on creation yields the correct result:
```
# podman container create --name testing --blkio-weight 50 registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/leap /bin/sh -c 'sleep 3600'
# podman  inspect -f '{{.HostConfig.BlkioWeight}}' testing
50
```

>No. I just expect it to work like Docker, which updates the HostConfig.

Note from the [man page](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-update.1.html#description):

> These new options are non-persistent and only last for the current execution of the container; the configuration will be honored on its next run. This means that this command can only be executed on an already running container and the changes made will be erased the next time the container is stopped and restarted, this is to ensure immutability.

I have no idea about update the update/cgroup code so I cannot really comment on real issue, just dropping this info so the inspect part is likely  expected.

> Note from the [man page](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-update.1.html#description):
> 
> > These new options are non-persistent and only last for the current execution of the container; the configuration will be honored on its next run. This means that this command can only be executed on an already running container and the changes made will be erased the next time the container is stopped and restarted, this is to ensure immutability.
> 
> I have no idea about update the update/cgroup code so I cannot really comment on real issue, just dropping this info so the inspect part is likely expected.

Hm, this still feels like a bug to me to be frankly honest: I as a user would expect that even if the cgroup settings are not persistent, that they are still visible when I inspect the container.

Currently the behavior is a bit odd, because I as the user have really no way of checking if I have applied any additional settings to a container after I have launched it, as `inspect` will never show these.
I am not disagreeing, just wanted to point to the docs. Why it was done that way? I do not know, you can look up the git/githup history to find out the reasons.
The update will not update the json file, since it only does the change temporarily, but it would be nice if inspect got the active data.

> The update will not update the json file, since it only does the change temporarily, but it would be nice if inspect got the active data.

It would be possible to retrieve some of the data from the cgroup settings, but I think it would be much easier to update the containers specgen and save it somewhere alongside the json (or somewhere else). What do you think?
@mheon PTAL

We do not save or store the changes made to resource limits, so we can't pull them back up after; it's an architectural limitation of our current database.

We could change the resource limit code in `podman inspect` to pull the limits from the actual container cgroup instead of the DB. That would solve things, but would add a lot of complexity, and likely be a lot slower than the current approach.

> We could change the resource limit code in `podman inspect` to pull the limits from the actual container cgroup instead of the DB. That would solve things, but would add a lot of complexity, and likely be a lot slower than the current approach.

That doesn't sound too compelling, to be honest. Wouldn't it make more sense to modify the database to also store the temporary resource limits alongside the persistent ones?
well that is a fundamental change to what we have been doing and needs a lot more thought, and will take a lot longer.  Potentially requiring a new majore version to podman 5.0.

Modifying resource limits in-place is on the list for the sqlite DB rewrite. I don't see it happening with boltdb, the structure of Podman right now is fundamentally built around immutable containers, and the way `update` works right now is by ignoring that and updating only the OCI runtime, not Podman.

I see. Do you have a rough idea when this sqlite DB rewrite is going to happen and whether it's worth it to refactor inspect to reflect the current container state? To me that sounds like an awful lot of work for an outcome that will land at some point anyway. But I definitely lack a lot of insight into podman's internals…

We don't have an exact timeframe, but probably first half of this year?

Refactoring inspect would be a sizable amount of work given the complexity (you need to support cgroups v1 and v2 both, and the various cgroups modes that we support (split cgroups, for example). So I'm going to say "not worth it" unless you have serious time constraints.

> Refactoring inspect would be a sizable amount of work given the complexity (you need to support cgroups v1 and v2 both, and the various cgroups modes that we support (split cgroups, for example). So I'm going to say "not worth it" unless you have serious time constraints.

Sounds reasonable to me. Since there are really no time constraints from my side, I have no intention to work on this given the required amount of work.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This is being worked on.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@giuseppe PTAL

If `container update` is meant to be volatile as it is documented, I don't see why `podman inspect` should show the volatile settings.  If you want to recreate the container from the `podman inspect` data you'll need the static configuration, not the transient state.

On the other hand, I'd expect `podman stats` to show the effective limits from the cgroup.
Since we have the answer I am going to close.  Feel free to continue the conversation here.
